pattern: A suffix that already has no duplicate keeps that property when you shrink it further from the front, so there is one single earliest-starting point past which every suffix is duplicate-free, and the answer is just how many groups of three it takes to reach that point.
hints: Scanning from the right while remembering values already seen tells you the first position (from the right) where a repeat would occur if you started the array there.
Every suffix starting at or after that position is automatically duplicate-free, and every suffix starting before it is not -- there is exactly one boundary, not several to compare.
Once you know the earliest safe starting index, the number of times you must remove a leading group of three (or fewer) to reach or pass it is just that index divided by three, rounded up.

edge_inputs.description: statement example two, the whole array is removed
statement example three, no operations needed
the only duplicate sits right at the very front
the only duplicate sits right at the very back, forcing a full extra operation
every value identical at the maximum allowed magnitude

stress.description: random arrays with a moderate value range so duplicates are common
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, max(2, n // 3)) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: mostly distinct arrays with a few duplicates seeded near the end
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = list(range(1, n + 1))
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    for _ in range(min(5, n // 2)):
        i = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        j = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        nums[i] = nums[j]
    return [nums]
